title: 8 Best Craigslist Alternatives in 2025 That Are Actually Better
metaDescription: Craigslist works—barely. Here are 8 better alternatives in 2025, ranked by safety, fees, and ease of use. #1 gives you $40 to start.
heroImageAlt: A split screen showing an old-style classified ad website versus a modern, clean marketplace app with colorful product listings
content:
8 Best Craigslist Alternatives in 2025 That Are Actually Better

Craigslist has been around since 1995, and honestly, it shows. The website looks the same as it did when flip phones were cutting-edge technology, scam listings are a daily hazard, and every transaction still requires the nerve to hand cash to a stranger in a parking lot. If you've ever thought "there has to be a better way," you're right—there are eight of them, and we've ranked them all.

Quick Comparison: The 8 Best Craigslist Alternatives

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly 10% (max $20) Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Overall
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes Tied to FB profile Popular but privacy issues
OfferUp 12.9% min $1.99 Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Good local app
Nextdoor Free Cash Yes No Neighborhood-verified Very local, very safe
Mercari 10% Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Easy shipping
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Global reach
Poshmark $2.95 / 20% Cash / card No Yes Account required Fashion niche
Letgo (via OfferUp) 12.9% Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Now merged into OfferUp

#1 Swapedly — Better Than Craigslist in Every Way That Matters

Craigslist's biggest appeal is that it's free. But free isn't the same as frictionless, and Craigslist introduces friction at every turn — no payment processing, no shipping, no buyer protection, and a scam problem that's genuinely exhausting. Swapedly addresses all of these without charging you an arm and a leg to do it.

The platform's Swap Bucks (SB) currency eliminates the awkward cash exchange. You list an item, someone buys it, you earn SB (1 SB = $1), and you spend those SB on anything else on the platform. It sounds like a simple idea, but it fundamentally changes how secondhand selling feels. There's no more nervously counting twenties in your car before driving away.

Fees are 10% capped at $20. On a $300 TV, that's $30. On a $500 treadmill, that's $20. The fee cap is genuinely unusual — most platforms keep charging the same percentage regardless of price. And unlike Craigslist, every transaction on Swapedly has built-in protection for buyers and sellers.

New users get 40 Swap Bucks ($40 value) just for signing up. You can grab your gift card at swapedly.com. That's a better welcome bonus than any of the alternatives on this list offer.

Sellers decide whether to offer local pickup, shipping, or both. No pressure either way. And because Swapedly doesn't require a Facebook, Google, or social media account, your privacy stays intact. You're a seller, not a data point.

Swapedly Pros

  • Fee cap at $20 — saves significantly on higher-value items
  • $40 Swap Bucks new user bonus
  • No cash handling, no awkward meetup payments
  • Privacy-first: no social account required
  • Buyer and seller protection built in
  • Local pickup and shipping both available

Swapedly Cons

  • Swap Bucks can't be withdrawn as cash
  • Newer platform — smaller listing volume than Craigslist

#2 Facebook Marketplace — The Craigslist Heir That Comes With Baggage

Facebook Marketplace is where most of Craigslist's user base migrated, and for understandable reasons: it has an actual app, better photos, and messaging that doesn't feel like emailing someone from 2003. Local transactions are still free, and the sheer number of listings in any given metro area is impressive.

But the privacy cost is real. Every transaction is tied to your personal Facebook profile, complete with your name, photo, and mutual friends. Some people find this reassuring. Others find it deeply uncomfortable. Scams are also still rampant — the platform size that makes it useful also makes it impossible to police effectively.

Facebook Marketplace Pros

  • Massive user base and listing volume
  • Free for local transactions
  • Easy to browse geographically

Facebook Marketplace Cons

  • Tied to your personal Facebook identity
  • Scams are a constant issue
  • 5% fee for shipped items

#3 OfferUp — The Clean Local Marketplace App

OfferUp is what you'd get if you took the good parts of Craigslist and rebuilt them with a competent design team. The app is genuinely nice to use, the photo interface is clean, and the location-based browsing works well. After merging with Letgo in 2020, it became the dominant local marketplace app in most US cities.

The 12.9% fee on shipped transactions is a drawback, especially compared to Swapedly's capped 10%. But for local cash deals, OfferUp is free and well-designed. It's a solid choice if you're selling locally and prefer face-to-face exchanges.

OfferUp Pros

  • Excellent mobile app design
  • Free for local cash transactions
  • Large user base in most cities

OfferUp Cons

  • 12.9% shipping fee with $1.99 minimum
  • Cash-only local deals mean no protection
  • Higher fee than Swapedly for shipped items

#4 Nextdoor — The Neighborhood-First Alternative

Nextdoor is the safest option on this list in terms of verified identities — everyone is confirmed to their actual neighborhood address. That means the person buying your old lawn mower is literally your neighbor, which makes for very low-risk transactions.

The limitation is obvious: it's purely local, purely cash, and purely within a few square miles. It's great for heavy furniture, free items, or things you'd rather give to a neighbor than a stranger from three cities away. Not great for anything requiring broader reach.

Nextdoor Pros

  • Neighborhood-verified accounts — extremely safe
  • Zero fees, zero friction for local deals
  • Great community reputation

Nextdoor Cons

  • Local only — no shipping, no national reach
  • Cash transactions only, no payment protection
  • Very limited for anything but neighborhood selling

#5 Mercari — The Shipping-Friendly Craigslist Alternative

Mercari shines when you want to ship items without the complexity of eBay. The listing process is fast, the prepaid label system is convenient, and the 10% seller fee is predictable. For smaller items under $200, it's one of the easier platforms to use.

Above $200, Swapedly's fee cap starts looking much more attractive. Mercari's 10% on a $400 item is $40 — double what Swapedly's capped fee would be. Also worth noting: buyers pay an additional service fee, which sometimes sours negotiations.

Mercari Pros

  • Simple, fast listing process
  • Good prepaid shipping label integration
  • Solid buyer and seller protection

Mercari Cons

  • No fee cap — gets expensive on higher-value items
  • Buyers pay additional fees beyond listed price
  • Slower dispute resolution than ideal

#6 eBay — Still the King for Specialty Items

If you're selling something rare, vintage, or niche — vintage cameras, sports memorabilia, rare books — eBay is still the best place to find buyers willing to pay real money. The auction format is underrated for items where demand determines price.

For everyday selling though? The 13.25% final value fee, the complex listing interface, and the aggressive policies toward sellers make it more hassle than it's worth for most people.

eBay Pros

  • Largest global buyer pool
  • Excellent for rare or collectible items
  • Auction format can drive higher prices

eBay Cons

  • 13.25% fee — the highest on this list
  • Complex, intimidating for casual sellers
  • Policies often favor buyers over sellers

#7 Poshmark — The Fashion-Only Craigslist Alternative

Poshmark is worth mentioning if fashion is your primary category. The community is engaged, listings get real traffic, and the social sharing features can boost visibility quickly. But the 20% fee above $15 is steep, and it's entirely shipping-only — no local pickup at all.

Poshmark Pros

  • Strong fashion buyer community
  • Social features increase visibility
  • Reliable payment and shipping system

Poshmark Cons

  • 20% commission is brutal
  • Fashion only — limited for general goods
  • No local pickup

#8 Letgo (Now Part of OfferUp)

Letgo was once a legitimate Craigslist competitor with a clean interface and solid local focus. In 2020, OfferUp acquired it and merged the platforms. If you had a Letgo account, it's now an OfferUp account. The brand effectively no longer exists independently, but the OfferUp platform absorbed its best features.

Letgo/OfferUp Pros

  • Clean app from Letgo's design legacy
  • Combined user base is now larger
  • Free for local transactions

Letgo/OfferUp Cons

  • Letgo as a brand no longer exists
  • OfferUp's 12.9% shipping fee applies
  • Some users lost listings in the merger

The Verdict: Which Craigslist Alternative Should You Actually Use?

Craigslist had a good run. But in 2025, there's simply no reason to accept its limitations when better alternatives exist. For most sellers — whether you're clearing out a garage or building a side income — Swapedly offers the best combination of low fees, privacy, and a smart currency system that turns every sale into buying power.

If you're selling locally and just want cash, Nextdoor or Facebook Marketplace will work. If you're in fashion, consider Poshmark. But if you want a platform that treats you like a person and not a data source, Swapedly is the answer.

Don't forget: new users get $40 in Swap Bucks free. That's real purchasing power, right from day one. Check out our Facebook Marketplace alternatives guide or our roundup of the best OfferUp alternatives if you want to keep comparing.
